note: Verbatim phrases of the locked narration, in spoken order, split at the script's <break> tags and sentence boundaries (captions <= ~95 chars). Block windows were measured by FFT cross-correlation of each WC-10-02-sNN.mp3 against the joined master (8 kHz mono PCM, numpy rfft, energy-normalized): the Sigma(ffprobe audio+0.4) formula predicts 181.022s but the ffprobed master is 180.611s (0.41s off, past the +/-0.3s gate). Normalized cross-correlation shows the master is the 12 blocks concatenated with an exact 0.400s gap after each (match score 1.000 every block); per-file ffprobe durations are inflated ~0.04s each by mp3 encoder-delay padding that the PCM concat strips, and the error accumulates (~0.41s by s12), which is the whole discrepancy. Each block startS is its measured onset; final end = ffprobed master duration 180.611s.

phrases.text: This is Support Tickets, the queue your team works inside WashCentral.
It's the platform's own help desk, separate from the cross-customer view in the last lesson.
Everything about the day's support load lives on this one screen.
Start at the top.
Four counters frame the backlog before you read a single row:
what's open, what's in progress, what closed today,
and how long the average ticket takes to resolve.
One look tells you whether the queue is calm or backed up.
Now the left panel.
This is the twelve-month trend, not today's numbers.
Volume is climbing, up seven percent over the year.
Handy context when a client asks whether their run of issues is unusual.
The middle panel breaks tickets down by type.
Billing leads, then technical, then feedback and general.
When you're deciding where the team's hours go,
this is the split that says billing questions are the heaviest load right now.
On the right, the health panel.
Most of the queue is already resolved, a smaller share in progress, and a thin band still open.
Read across, it's a quick pulse on whether work is getting closed or piling up.
Below the summary sits the list itself.
This is where you actually work.
Two buttons ride the top corner:
one opens a brand-new ticket, the other exports the list.
We'll open a new one shortly.
We never export while recording.
Above the table, a search box and a filter control.
Type a ticket number or a few words of the subject to jump straight to it,
or open the filters to narrow by field.
On a busy queue, this is how you find one ticket fast.
Read the column headers.
Ticket number, subject, location, priority, status, owner, and created date.
The two you'll lean on most are priority, for what to grab first,
and the owner, for who already has it.
Now the rows.
A POS receipt problem in progress, a billing dispute still open,
a membership that won't activate, a tunnel emergency-stop already resolved.
Priority runs from critical down to medium,
and each ticket names a person or a team on the hook for it.
At the foot of the list, the page controls.
Rows per page, a simple counter, and pager arrows.
One quirk worth knowing: the card says eighteen records while only four load here.
In the demo tenant, treat those four as samples and the counts as stand-ins.
Now open a new ticket.
This is the one write action in the lesson.
Open the blank form, look it over, then back out without saving and without touching a field.
We're documenting where a new ticket starts, not filing one.
That's the whole page.
The counters give you the backlog,
the middle panels tell you what kind of work it is and how much is cleared,
and the list hands you each ticket with its priority and its owner.
Enough to triage before you open a single one.
